catalog.name: Crab Nebula
catalog.funFact: The remnant of a supernova explosion observed by Chinese astronomers in 1054 AD.

catalog.name: Aquarius Globular Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of the oldest and most metal-poor globular clusters, estimated at 13 billion years old.

catalog.name: Canes Venatici Globular
catalog.funFact: Contains about 500,000 stars — one of the finest globular clusters in the northern hemisphere.

catalog.name: Cat's Eye Globular
catalog.funFact: The closest globular cluster to Earth at about 7,200 light-years away.

catalog.name: Rose Cluster
catalog.funFact: Often considered the finest globular cluster in the northern sky, containing over 100,000 stars.

catalog.name: Butterfly Cluster
catalog.funFact: Its shape resembles a butterfly with outstretched wings and contains about 80 stars.

catalog.name: Ptolemy Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of the most prominent open clusters, described by Ptolemy in 130 AD and visible to the naked eye.

catalog.name: Lagoon Nebula
catalog.funFact: A giant interstellar cloud and active stellar nursery spanning 110 by 50 light-years.

catalog.name: Ophiuchus Globular I
catalog.funFact: One of the nearer globular clusters to the galactic center, about 25,800 light-years away.

catalog.name: Ophiuchus Globular II
catalog.funFact: A rich globular cluster located about 14,300 light-years from Earth.

catalog.name: Wild Duck Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of the most densely populated open clusters known, containing nearly 3,000 stars.

catalog.name: Gumball Globular
catalog.funFact: A loosely concentrated globular cluster located about 15,700 light-years away.

catalog.name: Great Hercules Cluster
catalog.funFact: In 1974, a radio message was beamed toward M13 from Arecibo. It will arrive in about 25,000 years.

catalog.name: Ophiuchus Globular III
catalog.funFact: Contains several hundred thousand stars and is rich in RR Lyrae variable stars.

catalog.name: Pegasus Globular
catalog.funFact: One of the densest globular clusters known, possibly containing a central intermediate-mass black hole.

catalog.name: Eagle Nebula
catalog.funFact: Home of the Pillars of Creation, the famous Hubble Space Telescope photograph taken in 1995.

catalog.name: Omega Nebula
catalog.funFact: Also known as the Swan Nebula — one of the brightest and most massive star-forming regions in the Milky Way.

catalog.name: Sagittarius Sparse Cluster
catalog.funFact: A relatively sparse open cluster located about 4,900 light-years from Earth.

catalog.name: Ophiuchus Globular IV
catalog.funFact: One of the most oblate globular clusters known, likely due to rapid rotation near the galactic bulge.

catalog.name: Trifid Nebula
catalog.funFact: Its name means divided into three lobes; it contains emission, reflection, and dark nebula regions.

catalog.name: Sagittarius Young Cluster
catalog.funFact: A young open cluster estimated to be only about 4.6 million years old.

catalog.name: Sagittarius Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of the brightest globular clusters visible from Earth and the first to be discovered, in 1665.

catalog.name: Sagittarius Rich Cluster
catalog.funFact: Contains about 150 stars spread over an area nearly the size of the full moon.

catalog.name: Sagittarius Star Cloud
catalog.funFact: The densest concentration of individual stars visible to the naked eye from Earth.

catalog.name: Sagittarius Rich Cluster II
catalog.funFact: Contains the Cepheid variable star U Sagittarii, used historically to measure cosmic distances.

catalog.name: Dumbbell Nebula
catalog.funFact: The first planetary nebula ever discovered, found by Charles Messier in 1764.

catalog.name: Sagittarius Globular
catalog.funFact: Contains the first millisecond pulsar ever discovered in a globular cluster.

catalog.name: Capricornus Globular
catalog.funFact: Has undergone core collapse — one of about 20 such post-collapse clusters known in the Milky Way.

catalog.name: Andromeda Galaxy
catalog.funFact: The nearest large galaxy — on a collision course with the Milky Way. They will merge in ~4.5 billion years.

catalog.name: Le Gentil Galaxy
catalog.funFact: A compact elliptical satellite galaxy of Andromeda, one of the closest ellipticals to Earth.

catalog.name: Triangulum Galaxy
catalog.funFact: Third largest galaxy in the Local Group — the most distant object visible to the naked eye under perfect skies.

catalog.name: Perseus Open Cluster
catalog.funFact: A bright open cluster of about 100 stars, visible to the naked eye under dark skies.

catalog.name: Gemini Open Cluster
catalog.funFact: A large bright open cluster with about 500 stars, easily resolved through binoculars.

catalog.name: Pinwheel Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of three Messier open clusters in Auriga, located about 4,100 light-years away.

catalog.name: Salt and Pepper Cluster
catalog.funFact: The richest of the three Auriga Messier clusters, containing about 500 stars.

catalog.name: Starfish Cluster
catalog.funFact: Shows a distinctive starfish or pi-shaped pattern among its brighter stars.

catalog.name: Cygnus Open Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of the nearest open clusters at about 825 light-years, a loose group of about 30 stars.

catalog.name: Little Beehive Cluster
catalog.funFact: Located 4 degrees south of Sirius, it features a striking orange giant star at its center.

catalog.name: Orion Nebula
catalog.funFact: The nearest stellar nursery, just 1,344 light-years away — visible to the naked eye as a fuzzy star.

catalog.name: De Mairan's Nebula
catalog.funFact: Part of the Orion Nebula complex, separated from M42 by a dark lane of dust.

catalog.name: Beehive Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of the nearest open clusters — the Beehive contains over 1,000 confirmed member stars.

catalog.name: Pleiades
catalog.funFact: The Seven Sisters have been known since antiquity and appear in artwork from over 20,000 years ago.

catalog.name: Puppis Rich Cluster
catalog.funFact: Contains the planetary nebula NGC 2438 as a coincidental foreground object — a unique visual pairing.

catalog.name: Puppis Bright Cluster
catalog.funFact: Visible to the naked eye, notable for a pair of double stars near its center.

catalog.name: Hydra Open Cluster
catalog.funFact: Messier recorded wrong coordinates, so it was considered a missing object for nearly 200 years.

catalog.name: Virgo Brightest Galaxy
catalog.funFact: The brightest galaxy in the Virgo Cluster and the first member of that cluster to be discovered.

catalog.name: Heart-Shaped Cluster
catalog.funFact: Contains about 200 stars with a notable red giant near the cluster center.

catalog.name: Whirlpool Galaxy
catalog.funFact: The first galaxy recognized as having a spiral structure, observed by Lord Rosse in 1845.

catalog.name: Cassiopeia Cluster
catalog.funFact: A rich cluster of about 200 stars with a notable yellow supergiant near its center.

catalog.name: Summer Rose Star
catalog.funFact: Has one of the largest apparent diameters of any globular cluster with relatively low stellar density.

catalog.name: Ring Nebula
catalog.funFact: The iconic ring is actually a barrel-shaped cloud of gas being expelled by the dying central star.

catalog.name: Sunflower Galaxy
catalog.funFact: One of the first galaxies identified as having a flocculent spiral structure with patchy, discontinuous arms.

catalog.name: Black Eye Galaxy
catalog.funFact: Has a striking dark band of absorbing dust in front of its bright nucleus giving it a distinctive appearance.

catalog.name: Leo Triplet I
catalog.funFact: Part of the Leo Triplet group, showing dust lanes and asymmetry from interaction with its neighbors.

catalog.name: Leo Triplet II
catalog.funFact: Asymmetric spiral arms distorted by gravitational interaction with M65 and NGC 3628.

catalog.name: Phantom Galaxy
catalog.funFact: A perfect face-on grand design spiral — stunning but challenging due to its very low surface brightness.

catalog.name: Cetus Seyfert Galaxy
catalog.funFact: One of the largest and brightest Seyfert galaxies, powered by a supermassive active black hole.

catalog.name: Orion Reflection Nebula
catalog.funFact: The brightest reflection nebula in the sky, glowing by reflected light from two embedded stars.

catalog.name: Lepus Globular
catalog.funFact: Unusual position in the anti-galactic center direction, possibly captured from a dwarf galaxy.

catalog.name: Bode's Galaxy
catalog.funFact: One of the brightest galaxies visible from Earth, interacting with M82 just 300,000 light-years away.

catalog.name: Cigar Galaxy
catalog.funFact: The most intensely star-forming galaxy in the nearby universe, producing stars 10 times faster than the Milky Way.

catalog.name: Southern Pinwheel
catalog.funFact: One of the closest bright barred spirals, hosting six observed supernovae — more than any other galaxy.

catalog.name: Virgo A
catalog.funFact: Home of the first black hole ever imaged (Event Horizon Telescope, 2019) — mass of 6.5 billion suns.

catalog.name: Hercules Globular II
catalog.funFact: Often overshadowed by M13, but actually one of the finest globular clusters in the sky.

catalog.name: Owl Nebula
catalog.funFact: Named for two dark patches resembling owl eyes — the nebula formed about 6,000 years ago.

catalog.name: Mirror Galaxy
catalog.funFact: One of the first targets of corrected Hubble optics in 1993, revealing individual Cepheid variable stars.

catalog.name: Pinwheel Galaxy
catalog.funFact: A near-perfect face-on spiral twice the diameter of the Milky Way with a highly asymmetric structure.

catalog.name: Sombrero Galaxy
catalog.funFact: One of the most photographed galaxies — its prominent dust lane gives it the appearance of a Mexican hat.

catalog.name: Canes Venatici Spiral
catalog.funFact: Has two extra spiral arms made of hot gas rather than stars, visible only in radio and X-ray.

catalog.name: Chi Persei (Double Cluster W)
catalog.funFact: Western member of the famous Double Cluster in Perseus, containing hundreds of young blue supergiant stars.

catalog.name: H Persei (Double Cluster E)
catalog.funFact: Eastern member of the Double Cluster, slightly younger and richer in red supergiant stars than NGC 869.

catalog.name: 47 Tucanae
catalog.funFact: The second brightest globular cluster, appearing to the naked eye as a fuzzy star next to the SMC.

catalog.name: Centaurus A
catalog.funFact: The nearest radio galaxy and active galactic nucleus, with a prominent dark dust lane across its center.

catalog.name: Omega Centauri
catalog.funFact: The largest globular cluster in the Milky Way with 10 million stars — likely a captured dwarf galaxy nucleus.

catalog.name: Jewel Box Cluster
catalog.funFact: Named for its colorful jewel-like stars. One of the youngest known open clusters at just 14 million years.

catalog.name: Carina Nebula
catalog.funFact: Four times larger than the Orion Nebula — it contains the massive and unstable star Eta Carinae.

catalog.name: Tarantula Nebula
catalog.funFact: If as close as Orion, it would cast visible shadows on Earth. Located in the Large Magellanic Cloud.

catalog.name: Helix Nebula
catalog.funFact: The largest apparent planetary nebula in the sky, sometimes called the Eye of God.

catalog.name: Eastern Veil Nebula
catalog.funFact: Part of the Cygnus Loop supernova remnant — the explosion occurred about 10,000-20,000 years ago.

catalog.name: Rosette Cluster
catalog.funFact: The central cluster of the Rosette Nebula — its stellar winds have blown a cavity in the surrounding gas.

catalog.name: California Nebula
catalog.funFact: Named for its striking resemblance to the state of California, ionized primarily by the star Xi Persei.

catalog.name: North America Nebula
catalog.funFact: Resembles the continent of North America; the Gulf of Mexico region contains the densest star formation.

catalog.name: Heart Nebula
catalog.funFact: Named for its heart shape, it emits brilliant hydrogen-alpha red from a large stellar nursery.

catalog.name: Soul Nebula
catalog.funFact: Also called the Embryo Nebula — a massive star-forming complex adjacent to the Heart Nebula.

catalog.name: Sculptor Galaxy
catalog.funFact: One of the brightest galaxies beyond our Local Group and one of the closest starburst galaxies.

catalog.name: Needle Galaxy
catalog.funFact: The archetypal edge-on spiral — its prominent dust lane and central bulge are textbook examples.

catalog.name: Whale Galaxy
catalog.funFact: Named for its resemblance to a whale, surrounded by a huge halo of hot gas from supernova explosions.

catalog.name: Deer Lick Galaxy
catalog.funFact: Often called a Milky Way twin; its central bulge rotates opposite to its disk — still unexplained.

catalog.name: Caroline's Rose
catalog.funFact: Discovered by Caroline Herschel in 1783, it contains ~1,000 stars in sweeping rose-petal arcs.

catalog.name: Hidden Galaxy
catalog.funFact: Would be one of the most spectacular sky objects if not hidden behind our galaxy's dust plane.

catalog.name: Hamburg Galaxy
catalog.funFact: Third member of the Leo Triplet, seen exactly edge-on with a dramatic dust lane bisecting it lengthwise.

catalog.name: Caldwell 7
catalog.funFact: The first galaxy outside the Local Group in which individual Cepheid variable stars were resolved.

catalog.name: Crescent Nebula
catalog.funFact: Formed by a fast stellar wind from Wolf-Rayet star WR 136 colliding with a slower earlier wind.

catalog.name: Helix (secondary)
catalog.funFact: The dying Sun-like star at its center will eventually become a white dwarf — our Sun's fate in 5 billion years.

catalog.name: Croc's Eye Galaxy
catalog.funFact: Has two concentric ring structures — an inner starburst ring and a faint outer ring rarely photographed.

catalog.name: Hydra Globular Cluster
catalog.funFact: One of the nearer globular clusters discovered by Messier, about 33,600 light-years from Earth.

catalog.name: Cooling Tower Cluster
catalog.funFact: A small but bright cluster near Sadr, the central star of Cygnus the Swan.

catalog.name: Lyra Globular
catalog.funFact: Located between Albireo and Sulafat, about 32,900 light-years from Earth.

catalog.name: Scorpius Dense Globular
catalog.funFact: One of the densest globular clusters known, hosting a remarkable number of blue straggler stars.

catalog.name: Sculptor Group Galaxy
catalog.funFact: An irregular Magellanic-type galaxy in the Sculptor Group, about 6.5 million light-years away.

catalog.name: Fornax A
catalog.funFact: A powerful radio galaxy in the Fornax Cluster, formed from a galaxy merger about 3 billion years ago.

catalog.name: Coma Globular
catalog.funFact: One of the more distant globular clusters from the galactic center, at about 58,000 light-years.
